memes.label: Archbishop of Canterbury
memes.description: "The Archbishop of Canterbury, whose institutional authority lends constitutional legitimacy to the baronial opposition against Edward II in Marlowe's play."

memes.label: Earl of Arundel
memes.description: "The Earl of Arundel, a royalist supporter whose fate illustrates the cost of loyalty to a failing king in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Baldock
memes.description: "Baldock, a scholar turned courtier whose career exemplifies the corruption of learning into service of power in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Banishment
memes.description: Banishment as a political tool and emotional wound — how the threat and reality of exile drives the conflict in Marlowe's Edward II.

memes.label: Battle of Boroughbridge
memes.description: "The Battle of Boroughbridge — the military climax of the baronial conflict, marking Edward's temporary triumph and Lancaster's defeat."

memes.label: Beaumont
memes.description: "Beaumont, a minor royal agent who delivers a warrant at Edward II's command in Act 1, emblematic of the loyal functionaries who execute the king's will without question."

memes.label: Berkeley (Lord Berkeley)
memes.description: Lord Berkeley, the reluctant jailer of Edward II — a baronial figure whose pity for the imprisoned king leads to his displacement by crueller gaolers.

memes.label: Berkeley Castle
memes.description: "Berkeley Castle, the site of Edward II's imprisonment and murder — the play's darkest location and the culmination of the tragedy's descent from court to dungeon."

memes.label: Bishop of Coventry
memes.description: "The Bishop of Coventry, humiliated and assaulted by Edward II in Act 1 for his role in Gaveston's exile, whose mistreatment becomes a symbol of Edward's disregard for ecclesiastical authority."

memes.label: Bishop of Winchester
memes.description: "The Bishop of Winchester, who serves the Mortimer faction in Act 5, delivering the crown to the Parliament and formally conveying Edward II's forced abdication."

memes.label: Boroughbridge
memes.description: "The battlefield at Boroughbridge in Yorkshire, where Edward II's royalist forces are decisively defeated and the Elder Spenser is captured by Rice ap Howel."

memes.label: Champion
memes.description: The King's Champion — a ceremonial figure who appears in the coronation pageantry, representing the ritual dimension of kingship that persists even as Edward's political authority collapses.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: Index of all thematic concepts explored in Christopher Marlowe's Edward II, from political theology to social critique.

memes.label: Council
memes.description: The council as an institution of governance in Edward II, serving alternately as a check on royal power, an instrument of faction, and the forum where the fate of the king is decided.

memes.label: Royal Court
memes.description: The royal court as a political stage and social institution in Edward II, where power is performed, favour is traded, and the conflict between the king and the nobility unfolds.

memes.label: Curse
memes.description: The language of cursing as dramatic speech-act — how curses in Edward II punctuate the play's most violent moments, from the Bishop's anathema to Edward's dying malediction.

memes.label: Deposition
memes.description: "The deposition of a lawful king as the ultimate political crisis in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.description: "- King Edward II"

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: Duty as a contested ethical concept in Edward II, where obligations to the crown, to noble honour, and to personal bonds collide and prove irreconcilable.

memes.label: Gilbert de Clare, Earl of Gloucester
memes.description: Gilbert de Clare, Earl of Gloucester — a baronial ally whose death before the play's action shapes its political landscape, his familial connection to the crown complicating his legacy.

memes.label: Edmund, Earl of Kent
memes.description: Half-brother to King Edward II — one of the few nobles who remains consistently loyal, mediating between king and barons while defending royal authority.

memes.label: Thomas, Earl of Lancaster
memes.description: Thomas, Earl of Lancaster — a leading baronial opponent of Edward II and Gaveston, whose resistance helps drive the play's political crisis.

memes.label: Ecclesiastical Power
memes.description: "The role of ecclesiastical authority in the political conflict of Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Edward II
memes.description: King Edward II (1284–1327), the tragic protagonist of Marlowe's play — a monarch whose personal attachments to Gaveston lead to political catastrophe, deposition, and murder.

memes.label: Edward II's Deposition
memes.description: "The deposition of Edward II — the constitutional crisis at the heart of Marlowe's play, where an anointed king is compelled to surrender his crown."

memes.label: The Murder of Edward II
memes.description: "The murder of Edward II at Berkeley Castle — the play's darkest moment and the act that transforms Mortimer from political actor to regicide."

memes.label: Elder Mortimer
memes.description: "The Elder Mortimer, uncle to the Younger Mortimer, a baronial leader whose grave counsel represents the older generation's political wisdom — and whose capture and ransom expose the limits of traditional authority."

memes.label: Elder Spenser
memes.description: "Elder Spenser, a loyalist military commander who fights for Edward II against the barons and is ultimately captured by Rice ap Howel fighting for Queen Isabella's forces."

memes.label: Event Index
memes.description: Index of all major events in Christopher Marlowe's Edward II, from Gaveston's return to Edward III's accession.

memes.label: Exile
memes.description: "Exile as political punishment, personal loss, and dramatic turning point in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Favoritism
memes.description: "The king's excessive favouritism towards Gaveston as a central political conflict in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Flattery
memes.description: "Flattery as a tool of court advancement and a symptom of political corruption in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Fortune
memes.description: "Fortune and the wheel of fortune as a governing metaphor for political rise and fall in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: France
memes.description: "France, the kingdom to which Queen Isabella and Prince Edward flee for refuge and from which they launch their military return to England against Edward II."

memes.label: Gaveston's Execution
memes.description: "The execution of Piers Gaveston by the barons — the play's first irreversible act, the point of no return, and the event that transforms political dispute into a cycle of vengeance."

memes.label: Gaveston's Exile
memes.description: "Gaveston's forced exile by the barons and bishops — the inciting incident of Marlowe's Edward II, where the conflict between royal favour and aristocratic privilege erupts into open political warfare."

memes.label: Gaveston's Marriage
memes.description: "Edward II's arrangement of Gaveston's marriage to his niece — a strategic move that elevates Gaveston into the royal family and intensifies baronial resentment of the king's favourite."

memes.label: Gaveston's Recall from Exile
memes.description: "Edward II's recall of Gaveston from exile — the inciting incident that sets the entire political catastrophe of Marlowe's play in motion."

memes.label: Grief
memes.description: "Grief as emotional response and political catalyst in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Gurney
memes.description: Co-jailer and accomplice in the murder of Edward II, Gurney appears alongside Matrevis as Mortimer's instrument in the imprisonment and degradation of the deposed king.

memes.label: Hainault
memes.description: "The County of Hainault, home territory of Sir John of Hainault, where Queen Isabella finds refuge and military support for her return to England against Edward II."

memes.label: Herald
memes.description: The Herald — an official messenger who delivers formal communications between the factions, embodying the protocols of political communication in Edward II.

memes.label: Honour Code
memes.description: "The chivalric honour code as a contested value system in Marlowe's Edward II, driving both baronial resistance and personal tragedy."

memes.label: James
memes.description: James — a minor figure who attends the imprisoned Edward II, whose small acts of compassion illuminate the human dimension of the deposition and its moral consequences.

memes.label: Kenilworth Castle
memes.description: "Kenilworth Castle, a major baronial fortress and the site of key military and political actions in the latter half of Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Edmund, Earl of Kent
memes.description: "Edmund, Earl of Kent (1301–1330), Edward II's loyal half-brother whose doomed attempt to rescue the deposed king leads to his execution."

memes.label: King of France
memes.description: The King of France — an external political actor whose support Queen Isabella seeks, representing the international dimension of the conflict in Edward II.

memes.label: Lancaster
memes.description: "Thomas, Earl of Lancaster (c.1278–1322), the senior baronial leader who leads the initial opposition to Gaveston and Edward II in Marlowe's play."

memes.label: Leicester
memes.description: A nobleman who serves as the queen's commissioner in Edward II, charged with taking custody of the deposed king and transferring him from Kenilworth to Berkeley Castle—a pivotal role in the machinery of deposition.

memes.label: Levune
memes.description: "Levune, a Frenchman who serves as intermediary between Queen Isabella and the French court, facilitating her return to England with military support against Edward II."

memes.label: Lightborn
memes.description: "Lightborn, the assassin employed by Mortimer to murder the deposed Edward II — a figure of chilling professional detachment in Marlowe's play."

memes.label: Love and Desire
memes.description: "Love and desire as destabilizing political forces in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: Marriage as a political institution in Edward II, where the union of Edward and Isabella serves as both dynastic necessity and the source of profound personal and political conflict.

memes.label: Matrevis
memes.description: One of Edward II's jailers and murderers, Matrevis is the more prominent of the two agents Mortimer commissions to imprison and ultimately kill the deposed king.

memes.label: Mortality
memes.description: "Death and mortality as thematic preoccupations and dramatic forces in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Mortimer's Execution
memes.description: "The arrest and execution of Younger Mortimer by the newly crowned Edward III — the play's final act of political violence that restores legitimate rule and closes the cycle of deposition and murder."

memes.label: The Niece
memes.description: The niece of Edward II and daughter of the Duke of Gloucester, married to Gaveston as part of Edward's strategy to embed his favourite within the nobility—a dynastic pawn whose marriage becomes a political flashpoint.

memes.label: Nobility
memes.description: The nobility as a social class and political force in Edward II, whose traditional privileges and martial identity come into conflict with Edward's personal rule and favouritism toward Gaveston.

memes.label: Parliament
memes.description: Parliament as a political institution in Edward II, representing baronial opposition to royal prerogative and serving as the constitutional counterweight to the king's personal rule.

memes.label: Aymer de Valence, Earl of Pembroke
memes.description: "Aymer de Valence, Earl of Pembroke, a moderate baronial figure whose attempt to occupy the middle ground proves impossible in the polarized world of Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.description: "Piers Gaveston (c.1284–1312), the king's favourite whose elevation and execution drive the central conflict of Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Poison
memes.description: Poison as a literal method of murder and a pervasive metaphor for corruption in Edward II, where the language of toxicity links personal betrayal to political decay.

memes.description: Imprisonment as both literal condition and political metaphor — how the language of confinement shapes the fall of kings, nobles, and favourites in Edward II.

memes.label: Queen Isabella
memes.description: "Queen Isabella (c.1295–1358), wife of Edward II, whose transformation from neglected spouse to political actor drives the latter half of Marlowe's play."

memes.label: Ransom
memes.description: The practice of ransoming captives as political and economic leverage — how the ransom dispute in Edward II exposes tensions between royal authority and baronial power.

memes.label: Rebellion

memes.description: "Revenge as a motivating passion and structural principle in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Rice ap Howel
memes.description: "Rice ap Howel, a Welsh commander fighting for Queen Isabella and Mortimer, who captures Elder Spenser at the battle of Boroughbridge and helps escort the deposed Edward II to his death."

memes.label: Royal Patronage
memes.description: "The system of royal patronage and court favour as a source of political conflict in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Sir John of Hainault
memes.description: A continental nobleman and ally of Queen Isabella in Edward II, Sir John of Hainault provides military support for the invasion of England and serves as a symbol of the international dimension of the baronial rebellion.

memes.label: Sovereignty
memes.description: "The nature and limits of royal sovereignty as contested between King Edward II and the baronial opposition in Marlowe's play."

memes.label: Execution of the Spensers
memes.description: "The capture and execution of both Spensers — the father and son who dominated Edward II's court — marking the collapse of the royalist faction and the consolidation of Mortimer and Isabella's power."

memes.label: Tower of London
memes.description: "The Tower of London, where the deposed Edward II is imprisoned and murdered by Lightborn in Act 5, transforming from a royal fortress into a site of regicide."

memes.label: Treason
memes.description: The charge of treason as a political weapon -- how accusations of treachery are wielded by crown and barons alike to delegitimise opponents and justify violence in Edward II.

memes.label: Trussel
memes.description: "Trussel, the parliamentary officer who formally demands Edward II's crown in Act 5, enacting the ritual of deposition with legalistic precision."

memes.label: Tynemouth
memes.description: "Tynemouth, the coastal fortress where Edward and Gaveston retreat from baronial pressure — a refuge that becomes a trap and the prelude to Gaveston's capture and execution."

memes.label: Guy de Beauchamp, Earl of Warwick
memes.description: "Guy de Beauchamp, Earl of Warwick (c.1272–1315), a baronial leader whose implacable hostility to Gaveston drives the early conflict and whose seizure and execution of the favourite escalates the political crisis."

memes.label: Westminster and London
memes.description: "Westminster and London, the seat of royal government and the primary setting for the court scenes of Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Wrath
memes.description: Wrath as a political and dramatic force in Edward II — royal anger, divine fury, baronial counter-wrath, and the destructive consequences of uncontrolled rage.

memes.label: Younger Spenser
memes.description: "The Younger Spenser, a courtier who rises through service to Gaveston and exemplifies the self-serving ambition of the court in Marlowe's Edward II."

memes.label: Younger Mortimer
memes.description: Roger Mortimer the younger (1287–1330), the baronial leader who evolves from defender of noble privilege to regicide — Edward II's most complex antagonist and tragic mirror.
